content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 ,J Street• Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Kara K. Ueda 
Best Best & Krieger, LLP 
500 Capitol Mall, Suite 1700 
Sacramento, CA 95814 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-104 
Dear Ms. Ueda: 
October 5, 2016 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Commissioner Noah Painter 
regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Refonn Act (the "Act") 1 and Section 
1090. Please note that we do not advise on any other area of law, including Public Contract Code or 
common law conflicts of interest. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (111 re 
Oglesby ( 1975) l FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and 
accurate. 
In regard to our advice on Section I 090, we are required to forward your request and all 
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Sacramento County 
District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section I 097. l(c)(3).) We did not receive a 
written response from either entity. (Section l097.l(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, 
for purposes of Section l 090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding 
against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097. l(c)(S).) 
QUESTION 
Do the conflict of interest provisions under Section 1090 or the Act prohibit a commissioner 
on the Sacramento Housing and Redevelopment Commission ("the Commission") from providing 
recommendations on financing contracts involving clients of his consulting business to specified 
government agencies? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. As explained in detail below, Section l 090 prohibits the commissioner from providing 
recommendations on financing contracts involving clients of his consulting business to specified 
government agencies. 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS2 
File No. A-16-104 
Page No. 2 
The Sacramento Housing and Redevelopment Agency (the "SHRA") is a joint powers 
authority of the City of Sacramento and the County of Sacramento. The City and County formed the 
SHRA to provide the constituent entities with a common staff and independent agency to oversee 
and implement their housing and redevelopment/community development programs and 
infrastructure. The SHRA owns and manages public housing in Sacramento, administers rental 
assistance vouchers, and uses a number of financing tools to expand housing opportunities. 
The financing tools provided by the SHRA include ''gap financing" which uses federal, 
state, and local funding to provide partial financing to affordable housing and other development 
projects that have already received traditional financing, but require additional funds to complete 
construction. Sources of"gap financing" include HUD HOME funds, HUD CDBG (Community 
Development Block Grant) funds, local housing trust funds from the city and/or county, and/or 
local affordable/mixed income housing funds from both the city and the county. Both the HUD 
Home funds and the HUD CDBG funds are made available to the SHRA annually and must be 
expended within a 24-month period or "funding cycle." If those funds are not expended within the 
funding cycle, they are returned to HUD by the SHRA. 
Funding for affordable housing development is also provided via the housing authorities for 
the City and County of Sacramento, known respectively as the Sacramento City Housing Authority 
and Sacramento County Housing Authority (collectively "housing authority" or "housing 
authorities"). The City Council and Board of Supervisors serve as the respective governing bodies 
for each housing authority. Among other responsibilities, the housing authorities oversee the issuing 
of bonds to provide tax-exempt loans in connection with affordable housing development. While 
both housing authorities exist within the SHRA, they operate independently of the SHRA in regards 
to bond financing. 
The last entity involved in the Sacramento Housing and Redevelopment financing scheme is 
the Sacramento Housing and Redevelopment Commission (the "Commission"). The Commission, 
which consists of eleven appointed members (five are appointed by the City Council and six by the 
Board of Supervisors), serves as the governing body of the SHRA and as an advisory body to the 
housing authorities, the Sacramento City Council, and the Sacramento County Board of 
Supervisors. The Commission has the authority to hire its own personnel, to sue and be sued, to 
receive and expend funds, acquire and dispose of property, and to enter into contracts for staff and 
employee services. 
While the Commission makes recommendations to these various other public agencies, its 
recommendations are not binding. However, the SHRA's staff has reviewed previous past gap 
financing recommendations made by the Commission and subsequent decisions by these other 
public agency boards following the Commission's recommendations. It is SHRA's position that the 
Commission makes substantive recommendations regarding gap financing and that over an 
extended period of time those recommendations have been regularly approved without significant 
amendment or modification by another government agency or agencies. 
1 The following facts are taken largely verbatim from the facts provided in your request and Lance Olson's 
email to the undersigned dated September 7, 2016. 


Approval Process for Bond Financing and "Gap Financing" 
File No. A-16-104 
Page No. 3 
As noted above, both the City and County of Sacramento have various tools for promoting 
affordable housing in the greater Sacramento Region. These tools include the above referenced 
"gap financing" and tax-exempt loans via bond financing. The application and review process for 
"gap financing'' and tax-exempt loans via bond financing are similar, but the processes differ in 
regards to the entities involved. 
For bond financing, the review process begins with the County and City housing authorities. 
Developers seeking tax exempt financing must submit a proposal to the appropriate housing 
authority based on the location of their project. For example, if a developer sought financing for a 
project located in the City of Sacramento, the developer would submit their application to the 
housing authority for the City of Sacramento. 
Once submitted, the relevant housing authority reviews the application and recommends 
whether or not to approve it. If the authority recommends approval, it prepares a financial 
instrument to finance the plan proposed in the application. The financing plan is then referred to the 
Commission for review, and the Commission submits its recommended action to the relevant 
legislative authority - either the City Council or the Board of Supervisors (depending on the 
project's location). 
The relevant legislative authority is not bound by the Commission's recommendation and is 
free to either approve or reject the financing plan. However, the Commission's recommendations 
are given serious consideration by the relevant legislative authorities and have been regularly 
approved. If the plan is approved, the housing authority issues the bond financing and provides 
funding to the applicant for the proposed project. There is no further review or appeal to another 
legislative body if the plan is rejected by the legislative authority. 
For "gap financing," the review process begins with the SHRA. Developers seeking "gap 
financing" must submit an application to the SHRA for review. Once received, an application is 
reviewed by SHRA staff and an initial detennination is made as to whether to recommend 
consideration by the Commission or to reject the application (e.g., if available funds have already 
been committed, then the SHRA staff would reject the application or more likely an application is 
rejected because it is incomplete, or does not comply with the Agency's adopted Multifamily 
Guidelines, or funding requirements). If the application is rejected, it receives no further review. 
Once the Commission reviews the application, its recommended action is provided to the 
relevant legislative authority who is not bound by the Commission's recommendation and is free to 
either approve or reject the financing. However, the Commission's recommendations are given 
serious consideration by the relevant legislative authorities and have been regularly approved by 
them. If financing is approved, the funds are then distributed by the Agency to the applicant. There 
is no further review or appeal to another legislative body if the plan proposal and bond financing are 
rejected by the legislative authority. 


Noah Painter and Domus Development. LLC 
File No. A-16-104 
Page No. 4 
Noah Painter is a member of the Commission. Commissioner Painter also started his own 
consulting finn, as a sole proprietor on August 2, 2016. With his finn, Commissioner Painter has 
the intention of assisting clients in both the political and real estate sectors. Commissioner Painter 
has been retained by Dom us Development, LLC ("Dom us") until December 2016. Dom us is a 
California limited liability company that develops affordable housing and infill mixed-use projects 
throughout California. Commissioner Painter's work with Domus is limited to two projects, one 
located in Sacramento County and the other in Yolo County. The project in Sacramento County will 
not involve financing from the SHRA, but will obtain its financing from state Cap and Trade funds. 
Commissioner Painter has one other client that may, from time to time, have business before the 
SHRA. That client is CFY Development. CFY has retained Commissioner Painter to provide 
advisory services and to assist them in structuring the financing for various projects in their 
portfolio. Some of these projects may come before the SHRA for funding requests. His remaining 
clients involve providing consulting services unrelated to his role on the Commission. 
Before starting his own consulting firm on August 2, 2016, Commissioner Painter was one 
of four Domus employees and served as a project manager for specific affordable housing projects. 
Domus is owned 2/3rds by Newport Partners LLC and I/3rd by Meea Kang (an individual). 
Newport employs approximately 20 people and provides accounting and asset management services 
to Domus. Newport and Ms. Kang also own Domus Management Company, which employs about 
100 people who provide property management services to properties owned by Newport. In effect, 
Newport is the holding company for Domus Development LLC, which acquires and develops 
affordable housing projects for the benefit of Newport. Domus Management Company then 
manages those properties. 
Commissioner Painter has no ownership interest in Domus Development LLC, Newport 
Partners LLC, or Domus Management Company. Domus does not have any funding applications 
currently pending before the Commission. Domus has secured "gap financing" from the SHRA in 
the past for its housing projects. SHRA staff estimate that Domus has sought financing for three 
projects in the past five years and has received HUD Home and HUD CDBG gap financing. 
According to Commissioner Painter, Domus would not likely use or accept in the future gap 
financing from the local housing trust funds or local affordable/mixed income housing funds. In 
addition, Commissioner Painter is not aware of any current plans by Domus to seek gap financing at 
all, but cannot rule out that possibility; thus, SHRA staff assumes it will receive funding 
applications from Domus in the future. Every application is considered on its own merits, and the 
receipt of financing in the past does not guarantee the granting of such funds in the future. 
According to Commissioner Painter, Domus does not intend to utilize bond financing from 
the housing authorities. In the event Domus identifies a project where bond financing makes sense, 
Commissioner Painter believes it will pursue that bond financing from other sources, not the local 
housing authorities. 


ANALYSIS 
Government Code Section 1090: 
File No. A-16-104 
Page No. 5 
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with 
financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from 
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their 
agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is intended "not only to strike at 
actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety." ( City of Imperial Beach v. 
Bailey ( 1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) 
Under Section I 090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has 
a financial interest." (People v. Honig ( 1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates 
Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call ( 1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) The prohibition applies 
regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. 
(Id. at pp. 646-649.) 
We employ a six-step analysis to determine whether an official has a disqualifying conflict 
of interest under Section 1090. 
Step One: Is the official subject to tlze provisions of Section 1090? 
Section I 090 applies to virtually all state and local officers, employees, and multi-member 
bodies, whether elected or appointed. The term "public official" is interpreted broadly under 
Section l 090 and even includes "independent contractors who perform a public function" and 
"whose official capacities carry the potential to exert considerable influence over the contracting 
decisions of a public agency." (See Hub City Solid Waste Services, Inc. v. City of Compton (2010) 
186 Cal.App.4th 1114, 1124-1125; citing California Housing Finance Agency v. Ha1101•er (2007) 
148 Cal.App.4th 682, 690-693. As a member of the Commission, which is the governing body of 
the SHRA, Commissioner Painter is considered a public official who must annually file a Statement 
of Economic Interests pursuant to the Act. (See https://www.shra.org.) Consistent with his status as 
a public official under the Act, and considering Section 1090's broad reach, Commissioner Painter 
is unquestionably subject to the provisions of Section 1090. 
Step Two: Does the decision at issue involve a contract? 
To determine whether a contract is involved in the decision, one may look to general 
principles of contract law (84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36 (2001 ); 78 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 230, 234 
( 1995)), while keeping in mind that "specific rules applicable to Sections 1090 and 1097 require 
that we view the transactions in a broad manner and avoid narrow and technical definitions of 
•contract.'" (People v. Honig, supra, at p. 351 citing Stigall, supra, at pp. 569, 571.) 


File No. A-16-104 
Page No. 6 
Here, the decisions at issue involve Commission recommendations to the City or County on 
potential contracts that involve bond financing or gap financing for developers seeking such funds. 
Accordingly, this step is met. 
Step Three: ls the official making or participating in ma/..ing a contract? 
Initially, it is important to note that Section l 090 reaches beyond the officials who actually 
execute the contract. Section l 090 casts a wide net to include those officials who participate in any 
way in the making of the contract: 
The decisional law, therefore, has not interpreted section 1090 in a 
hypertechnical manner, but holds that an official (or a public employee) 
may be convicted of violation no matter whether he actually participated 
personally in the execution of the questioned contract, if it is established 
that he had the opportunity to, and did, influence execution directly or 
indirectly to promote his personal interests. 
(People v. Sobel ( 1974) 40 Cal.App.3d 1046, 1052.) Therefore, participation in the making of a 
contract is defined broadly as any act involving preliminary discussions, negotiations, 
compromises, reasoning, planning, drawing of plans and specifications, and solicitation for bids. 
(Millbrae Assn. for Residential Survival v. City of Millbrae (l 968) 262 Cal.App.2d 222, 23 7; see 
also Stiga/1 v. City of Taft ( 1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) And individuals in advisory positions can 
influence the development of a contract during these early stages of the contracting process even 
though they have no actual power to execute the final contract. (See, e.g., Schaefer. supra. 140 
Cal.App.2d at p. 291; City Co1111cil v. McKinley ( 1978) 80 Cal.App.3d 204 [ member of Park and 
Recreation Board who owned a landscape architectural finn participated in the making of a contract 
in violation of Section 1090 where he was also a member of a committee created to advise the 
Board on the design, architecture, landscaping and technical planning of a Japanese garden]; see 
also 81 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 3 1 7, 3 1 8-19 ( 1998) [after leaving office and establishing a private 
business, Section 1090 precluded fonner council member from participating in City's loan program 
where he had been involved in the "planning, discussions, and approval necessary to implement 
[the] loan program" and "had the opportunity and did participate in the policy decision to create the 
government program under which the contract would later be executed"].) 
Here, in addition to serving as the governing body of the SHRA, the Commission serves as 
an advisory body to the housing authorities, the Sacramento City Council, and the Sacramento 
County Board of Supervisors. In this advisory capacity, the Commission makes recommendations 
to these public agencies as to whether they should approve contracts involving either gap financing 
or bond financing. And according to your facts, the Commission's recommendations "over an 
extended period of time . . .  have been regularly approved without significant amendment or 
modification by another government agency or agencies." Thus, although the Commission has no 
power to actually authorize or enter into these financing contracts, it has significantly influenced the 
ultimate execution of such contracts with its recommendations over the years. 
Based on these facts, we must conclude that Commissioner Painter participates in the 
making of contracts involving gap financing or bond financing where the Commission has provided 


File No. A-16-104 
Page No. 7 
"substantive recommendations" that are regularly approved by other agencies in a position to enter 
into such contracts. Accordingly, this step is satisfied. 
Step Four: Does the official have aji11a11cial interest in the contract? 
Under section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has 
a financial interest'' (People v. Honig, supra, 48 Cal.App.4th at p. 333), and officials are deemed to 
have a financial interest in a contract if they might profit from it in any way. (Ibid.) Although 
section 1090 nowhere specifically defines the term "financial interest," case law and Attorney 
General Opinions state that prohibited financial interests may be indirect as well as direct, and may 
involve financial losses, or the possibility of losses, as well as the prospect of pecuniary gain. 
( Thomson v. Call, supra, 38 Cal.3d at pp. 645, 651-652; see also People 1•. Vallerga ( 1977) 6 7 
Cal.App.3d 847, 867, fn. 5; Teny v. Bender (1956) 143 Cal.App.2d 198, 207-208; People v. Darby 
(1952) 114 Cal.App.2d 412, 431-432; 85 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36-38 (2002); 84 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 161-162 (2001).) 
In addition, case law and statutory exceptions to Section 1090 make clear that the tenn 
"financially interested" must be liberally interpreted. (See, e.g., People v. Deysher (1934) 
2 Cal.2d 141, 146 [''(h)owever devious and winding the chain may be which connects the officer 
with the forbidden contract, if it can be followed and the connection made, the contract is void"].) 
Further, "the certainty of financial gain is not necessary to create a conflict of interest . .. (t)he 
government's right to the absolute, undivided allegiance of a public officer is diminished as 
effectively where the officer acts with a hope of personal financial gain as where he acts with 
certainty.'' (People v. Gnass (2002) 101 Cal.App.4th I 271, 1298 (citations omitted).) 
With these principles as a backdrop, we tum to the issue concerning the potential conflict of 
interest presented by this matter. In a 2003 Attorney General Opinion, the board of a redevelopment 
agency anticipated entering into contracts with business finns intending to locate or expand their 
offices in a certain redevelopment project area. (86 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 187 (2003).) Several of those 
businesses were clients of one of the board member's promotional products company. (Ibid.) In 
determining that the board member would have a financial interest in any contracts between the 
redevelopment agency and those businesses, the Opinion stated: 
The board member would have a "financial interest" in the 
contracts due to his business relationship with the contracting parties 
as a supplier of goods or services. (See 85 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 176, 
177-179 (2002); 85 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 35-37 (2002).) In this 
regard, the board member could be influenced by the prospect of 
future business opportunities directly related to the contracts or by a 
desire to maintain favorable ongoing relationships with the 
contracting parties. The Legislature has made clear that ongoing 
business relationships may represent financial interests for purposes 
of section 1090. (See, e.g., § 1091, subd. (b)(5), (b)(6), (b)(S).) The 
purpose of section 1090 "is to remove or limit the possibility of any 
personal influence, either directly or indirectly, which might bear 
upon an official's decision . . .. " (Stigall ,,. City of Taft (1962) 58 
Cal.2d 656, 569; Finnegan v. Schrader (2001) 91 Cal.App.4th 572, 


File No. A-16-104 
Page No. 8 
579-580; Tho1pe v. Long Beach Community College Dist. (2000) 83 
Cal.App.4th 655, 659; Frazer-Yamor Agency, Inc. v. County of Del 
Norte (1977) 68 Cal.App.3d 201, 215.) 
(86 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. at p. 1 88.) 
Here, Commissioner Painter owns his own consulting finn to assist clients in both the 
political and real estate sectors. He has been retained by Domus in this capacity until December of 
2016. Similar to the situation just described, there is a practical concern that he will be influenced 
by a desire to "maintain ongoing relationships" with Domus, and any other company he represents, 
seeking to contract with any of the relevant government agencies for financing. Indeed, it is difficult 
to imagine how he would ever be able to provide his absolute, undivided allegiance to the 
Commission as a public servant while participating in the contracting process involving his own 
clients. To be sure, a real potential exists for the councilmember to curry favor with his clients 
understanding that it may ultimately affect his ongoing consulting business with them and other 
future business opportunities. This is exactly the type of indirect interest Section 1090 attempts to 
thwart. (See, e.g., 88 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. I 06, I 07 (2005) citing Frazer-Yamor Agency, supra, 68 
Cal.App.3d at pp. 214-215 [California courts strictly enforce Section I 090, and have consistently 
voided contracts where they find the public officer has an indirect interest in the contract; indeed, 
even though an officer may not directly benefit from the tenns of the contract, it is significant that 
the contract will contribute to the financial health of the contracting party with which the officer is 
associated].) 
The conclusion that the councilmember has a financial interest in contracts between the 
relevant government agencies and the clients of his finn is further supported by looking to the 
statutory exceptions for Section I 090. (See 85 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34 (2002) [it is appropriate to 
look to the definitions of the remote and non-interest exceptions contained in sections l 091 and 
l 091.5 for guidance in detennining what falls within the scope of the tenn "financial interest" as 
used in Section I 090].) 
Turning to the statutory exceptions, under Section I 091 (b )(8), an official has a remote 
interest in a contract entered into by the body or board of which they are a member if they are a 
"supplier of goods or services when those goods or services have been supplied to the contracting 
party by the officer for at least five years prior to his or her election or appointment to office." 
Thus, an official would have a financial interest in a contract entered into by a government entity if 
he or she provides services to the party contracting with that government entity, but only a remote 
interest if those services have been provided for at least five years prior to his or her appointment to 
the relevant government entity. 
Here, as mentioned, Commissioner Painter owns a consulting business that provides various 
services to clients in the political and real estate sectors who currently may have or seek to have 
financing contracts with the relevant government agencies. The existence of the instant exception 
further supports the conclusion that he would have a financial interest in all instances where clients 
of his firm seek to enter into contracts with those agencies. We note, however, that because 
Commissioner Painter only began operating his business providing consulting services in August of 
20 I 6, the exception under Section 1091 (b )(8) would not apply. 


File No. A-1 6-104 
Page No. 9 
Based on the facts provided, Commissioner Painter would have a conflict of interest under 
Section 1 090 involving the recommendation of financing contracts between the relevant 
government agencies and clients of his consulting firm. As mentioned, the remote interest exception 
under Section 1091 (b)(8) does not apply in this situation and we do not find any other exceptions to 
be applicable.3 
Finally, we note that although Commissioner Painter has a conflict of interest in decisions 
involving Domus, and other clients of his firm, the Commission is not prohibited from making 
recommendations on financing so long as Commissioner Painter recuses himself.4 "An advisory 
committee may perform its responsibilities as long as the interested member abstains and does not 
participate in the giving of advice." (82 Ops. Cal. Atty. Gen. 126, 127 ( 1999) citing Cal. Atty. Gen., 
Indexed Letter No. IL 72-143 (Aug. 16, 1 992).) 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (9 I 6) 322-5660. 
SKB:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
By: 
Sukhi K. Brar 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division 
l We restrict our advice under Section I 090 10 only recommendations of the Commission that will directly 
involve clients of Commissioner Painter's consulting finn. We do not have sufficient infonnation on the circumstances 
surrounding a specific recommendation that does not involve one of his clients such that we can provide a thorough 
analysis under Section 1090. And we do not provide infonnal advice in this regard. 
4 In light of the conclusion that Commissioner Painter must recuse himself from participating in Commission 
recommendations involving financing for his clients, further advice under the Act is not necessary.

sections.question:
Do the conflict of interest provisions under Section 1090 or the Act prohibit a commissioner 
on the Sacramento Housing and Redevelopment Commission ("the Commission") from providing 
recommendations on financing contracts involving clients of his consulting business to specified 
government agencies?
sections.conclusion:
Yes. As explained in detail below, Section l 090 prohibits the commissioner from providing 
recommendations on financing contracts involving clients of his consulting business to specified 
government agencies. 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained inFACTS2  
The Sacramento Housing and Redevelopment Agency (the "SHRA") is a joint powers 
authority of the City of Sacramento and the County of Sacramento. The City and County formed the 
SHRA to provide the constituent entities with a common staff and independent agency to oversee 
and implement their housing and redevelopment/community development programs and 
infrastructure. The SHRA owns and manages public housing in Sacramento, administers rental 
assistance vouchers, and uses a number of financing tools to expand housing opportunities. 
The financing tools provided by the SHRA include ''gap financing" which uses federal, 
state, and local funding to provide partial financing to affordable housing and other development

construction. Sources of"gap financing" include HUD HOME funds, HUD CDBG (Community 
Development Block Grant) funds, local housing trust funds from the city and/or county, and/or 
local affordable/mixed income housing funds from both the city and the county. Both the HUD

expended within a 24-month period or "funding cycle." If those funds are not expended within the 
funding cycle, they are returned to HUD by the SHRA. 
Funding for affordable housing development is also provided via the housing authorities for 
the City and County of Sacramento, known respectively as the Sacramento City Housing Authority 
and Sacramento County Housing Authority (collectively "housing authority" or "housing 
authorities"). The City Council and Board of Supervisors serve as the respective governing bodies 
for each housing authority. Among other responsibilities, the housing authorities oversee the issuing 
of bonds to provide tax-exempt loans in connection with affordable housing development. While

extended period of time those recommendations have been regularly approved without significant 
amendment or modification by another government agency or agencies. 
1 The following facts are taken largely verbatim from the facts provided in your request and Lance Olson's 
email to the undersigned dated September 7, 2016. 


Approval Process for Bond Financing and "Gap Financing"  
As noted above, both the City and County of Sacramento have various tools for promoting 
affordable housing in the greater Sacramento Region. These tools include the above referenced 
"gap financing" and tax-exempt loans via bond financing. The application and review process for 
"gap financing'' and tax-exempt loans via bond financing are similar, but the processes differ in 
regards to the entities involved. 
For bond financing, the review process begins with the County and City housing authorities. 
Developers seeking tax exempt financing must submit a proposal to the appropriate housing 
authority based on the location of their project. For example, if a developer sought financing for a 
project located in the City of Sacramento, the developer would submit their application to the 
housing authority for the City of Sacramento. 
Once submitted, the relevant housing authority reviews the application and recommends 
whether or not to approve it. If the authority recommends approval, it prepares a financial 
instrument to finance the plan proposed in the application. The financing plan is then referred to the 
Commission for review, and the Commission submits its recommended action to the relevant 
legislative authority - either the City Council or the Board of Supervisors (depending on the 
project's location). 
The relevant legislative authority is not bound by the Commission's recommendation and is 
free to either approve or reject the financing plan. However, the Commission's recommendations

approved. If the plan is approved, the housing authority issues the bond financing and provides

legislative body if the plan is rejected by the legislative authority. 
For "gap financing," the review process begins with the SHRA. Developers seeking "gap 
financing" must submit an application to the SHRA for review. Once received, an application is

consideration by the Commission or to reject the application (e.g., if available funds have already

Guidelines, or funding requirements). If the application is rejected, it receives no further review. 
Once the Commission reviews the application, its recommended action is provided to the 
relevant legislative authority who is not bound by the Commission's recommendation and is free to 
either approve or reject the financing. However, the Commission's recommendations are given 
serious consideration by the relevant legislative authorities and have been regularly approved by 
them. If financing is approved, the funds are then distributed by the Agency to the applicant. There 
is no further review or appeal to another legislative body if the plan proposal and bond financing are 
rejected by the legislative authority.

Noah Painter is a member of the Commission. Commissioner Painter also started his own 
consulting finn, as a sole proprietor on August 2, 2016. With his finn, Commissioner Painter has 
the intention of assisting clients in both the political and real estate sectors. Commissioner Painter 
has been retained by Dom us Development, LLC ("Dom us") until December 2016. Dom us is a 
California limited liability company that develops affordable housing and infill mixed-use projects 
throughout California. Commissioner Painter's work with Domus is limited to two projects, one 
located in Sacramento County and the other in Yolo County. The project in Sacramento County will 
not involve financing from the SHRA, but will obtain its financing from state Cap and Trade funds. 
Commissioner Painter has one other client that may, from time to time, have business before the 
SHRA. That client is CFY Development. CFY has retained Commissioner Painter to provide 
advisory services and to assist them in structuring the financing for various projects in their 
portfolio. Some of these projects may come before the SHRA for funding requests. His remaining 
clients involve providing consulting services unrelated to his role on the Commission. 
Before starting his own consulting firm on August 2, 2016, Commissioner Painter was one 
of four Domus employees and served as a project manager for specific affordable housing projects. 
Domus is owned 2/3rds by Newport Partners LLC and I/3rd by Meea Kang (an individual). 
Newport employs approximately 20 people and provides accounting and asset management services 
to Domus. Newport and Ms. Kang also own Domus Management Company, which employs about 
100 people who provide property management services to properties owned by Newport. In effect, 
Newport is the holding company for Domus Development LLC, which acquires and develops 
affordable housing projects for the benefit of Newport. Domus Management Company then 
manages those properties. 
Commissioner Painter has no ownership interest in Domus Development LLC, Newport 
Partners LLC, or Domus Management Company. Domus does not have any funding applications 
currently pending before the Commission. Domus has secured "gap financing" from the SHRA in 
the past for its housing projects. SHRA staff estimate that Domus has sought financing for three 
projects in the past five years and has received HUD Home and HUD CDBG gap financing. 
According to Commissioner Painter, Domus would not likely use or accept in the future gap 
financing from the local housing trust funds or local affordable/mixed income housing funds. In 
addition, Commissioner Painter is not aware of any current plans by Domus to seek gap financing at 
all, but cannot rule out that possibility; thus, SHRA staff assumes it will receive funding 
applications from Domus in the future. Every application is considered on its own merits, and the 
receipt of financing in the past does not guarantee the granting of such funds in the future. 
According to Commissioner Painter, Domus does not intend to utilize bond financing from 
the housing authorities. In the event Domus identifies a project where bond financing makes sense, 
Commissioner Painter believes it will pursue that bond financing from other sources, not the local 
housing authorities.
sections.analysis:
Government Code Section 1090:  
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with 
financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from 
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their 
agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is intended "not only to strike at 
actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety." ( City of Imperial Beach v. 
Bailey ( 1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) 
Under Section I 090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has 
a financial interest." (People v. Honig ( 1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates 
Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call ( 1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) The prohibition applies 
regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. 
(Id. at pp. 646-649.) 
We employ a six-step analysis to determine whether an official has a disqualifying conflict 
of interest under Section 1090. 
Step One: Is the official subject to tlze provisions of Section 1090? 
Section I 090 applies to virtually all state and local officers, employees, and multi-member 
bodies, whether elected or appointed. The term "public official" is interpreted broadly under 
Section l 090 and even includes "independent contractors who perform a public function" and 
"whose official capacities carry the potential to exert considerable influence over the contracting 
decisions of a public agency." (See Hub City Solid Waste Services, Inc. v. City of Compton (2010) 
186 Cal.App.4th 1114, 1124-1125; citing California Housing Finance Agency v. Ha1101•er (2007) 
148 Cal.App.4th 682, 690-693. As a member of the Commission, which is the governing body of 
the SHRA, Commissioner Painter is considered a public official who must annually file a Statement 
of Economic Interests pursuant to the Act. (See https://www.shra.org.) Consistent with his status as 
a public official under the Act, and considering Section 1090's broad reach, Commissioner Painter 
is unquestionably subject to the provisions of Section 1090. 
Step Two: Does the decision at issue involve a contract? 
To determine whether a contract is involved in the decision, one may look to general 
principles of contract law (84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36 (2001 ); 78 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 230, 234 
( 1995)), while keeping in mind that "specific rules applicable to Sections 1090 and 1097 require 
that we view the transactions in a broad manner and avoid narrow and technical definitions of 
•contract.'" (People v. Honig, supra, at p. 351 citing Stigall, supra, at pp. 569, 571.)  
Here, the decisions at issue involve Commission recommendations to the City or County on 
potential contracts that involve bond financing or gap financing for developers seeking such funds. 
Accordingly, this step is met. 
Step Three: ls the official making or participating in ma/..ing a contract? 
Initially, it is important to note that Section l 090 reaches beyond the officials who actually 
execute the contract. Section l 090 casts a wide net to include those officials who participate in any 
way in the making of the contract: 
The decisional law, therefore, has not interpreted section 1090 in a 
hypertechnical manner, but holds that an official (or a public employee) 
may be convicted of violation no matter whether he actually participated 
personally in the execution of the questioned contract, if it is established 
that he had the opportunity to, and did, influence execution directly or 
indirectly to promote his personal interests. 
(People v. Sobel ( 1974) 40 Cal.App.3d 1046, 1052.) Therefore, participation in the making of a 
contract is defined broadly as any act involving preliminary discussions, negotiations, 
compromises, reasoning, planning, drawing of plans and specifications, and solicitation for bids. 
(Millbrae Assn. for Residential Survival v. City of Millbrae (l 968) 262 Cal.App.2d 222, 23 7; see 
also Stiga/1 v. City of Taft ( 1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) And individuals in advisory positions can 
influence the development of a contract during these early stages of the contracting process even 
though they have no actual power to execute the final contract. (See, e.g., Schaefer. supra. 140 
Cal.App.2d at p. 291; City Co1111cil v. McKinley ( 1978) 80 Cal.App.3d 204 [ member of Park and 
Recreation Board who owned a landscape architectural finn participated in the making of a contract 
in violation of Section 1090 where he was also a member of a committee created to advise the 
Board on the design, architecture, landscaping and technical planning of a Japanese garden]; see 
also 81 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 3 1 7, 3 1 8-19 ( 1998) [after leaving office and establishing a private 
business, Section 1090 precluded fonner council member from participating in City's loan program 
where he had been involved in the "planning, discussions, and approval necessary to implement 
[the] loan program" and "had the opportunity and did participate in the policy decision to create the 
government program under which the contract would later be executed"].) 
Here, in addition to serving as the governing body of the SHRA, the Commission serves as 
an advisory body to the housing authorities, the Sacramento City Council, and the Sacramento 
County Board of Supervisors. In this advisory capacity, the Commission makes recommendations 
to these public agencies as to whether they should approve contracts involving either gap financing 
or bond financing. And according to your facts, the Commission's recommendations "over an 
extended period of time . . .  have been regularly approved without significant amendment or 
modification by another government agency or agencies." Thus, although the Commission has no 
power to actually authorize or enter into these financing contracts, it has significantly influenced the 
ultimate execution of such contracts with its recommendations over the years. 
Based on these facts, we must conclude that Commissioner Painter participates in the 
making of contracts involving gap financing or bond financing where the Commission has provided  
"substantive recommendations" that are regularly approved by other agencies in a position to enter 
into such contracts. Accordingly, this step is satisfied. 
Step Four: Does the official have aji11a11cial interest in the contract? 
Under section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has 
a financial interest'' (People v. Honig, supra, 48 Cal.App.4th at p. 333), and officials are deemed to 
have a financial interest in a contract if they might profit from it in any way. (Ibid.) Although 
section 1090 nowhere specifically defines the term "financial interest," case law and Attorney 
General Opinions state that prohibited financial interests may be indirect as well as direct, and may 
involve financial losses, or the possibility of losses, as well as the prospect of pecuniary gain. 
( Thomson v. Call, supra, 38 Cal.3d at pp. 645, 651-652; see also People 1•. Vallerga ( 1977) 6 7 
Cal.App.3d 847, 867, fn. 5; Teny v. Bender (1956) 143 Cal.App.2d 198, 207-208; People v. Darby 
(1952) 114 Cal.App.2d 412, 431-432; 85 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36-38 (2002); 84 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 161-162 (2001).) 
In addition, case law and statutory exceptions to Section 1090 make clear that the tenn 
"financially interested" must be liberally interpreted. (See, e.g., People v. Deysher (1934) 
2 Cal.2d 141, 146 [''(h)owever devious and winding the chain may be which connects the officer 
with the forbidden contract, if it can be followed and the connection made, the contract is void"].) 
Further, "the certainty of financial gain is not necessary to create a conflict of interest . .. (t)he 
government's right to the absolute, undivided allegiance of a public officer is diminished as 
effectively where the officer acts with a hope of personal financial gain as where he acts with 
certainty.'' (People v. Gnass (2002) 101 Cal.App.4th I 271, 1298 (citations omitted).) 
With these principles as a backdrop, we tum to the issue concerning the potential conflict of 
interest presented by this matter. In a 2003 Attorney General Opinion, the board of a redevelopment 
agency anticipated entering into contracts with business finns intending to locate or expand their 
offices in a certain redevelopment project area. (86 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 187 (2003).) Several of those 
businesses were clients of one of the board member's promotional products company. (Ibid.) In 
determining that the board member would have a financial interest in any contracts between the 
redevelopment agency and those businesses, the Opinion stated: 
The board member would have a "financial interest" in the 
contracts due to his business relationship with the contracting parties 
as a supplier of goods or services. (See 85 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 176, 
177-179 (2002); 85 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 35-37 (2002).) In this 
regard, the board member could be influenced by the prospect of 
future business opportunities directly related to the contracts or by a 
desire to maintain favorable ongoing relationships with the 
contracting parties. The Legislature has made clear that ongoing 
business relationships may represent financial interests for purposes 
of section 1090. (See, e.g., § 1091, subd. (b)(5), (b)(6), (b)(S).) The 
purpose of section 1090 "is to remove or limit the possibility of any 
personal influence, either directly or indirectly, which might bear 
upon an official's decision . . .. " (Stigall ,,. City of Taft (1962) 58 
Cal.2d 656, 569; Finnegan v. Schrader (2001) 91 Cal.App.4th 572,  
579-580; Tho1pe v. Long Beach Community College Dist. (2000) 83 
Cal.App.4th 655, 659; Frazer-Yamor Agency, Inc. v. County of Del 
Norte (1977) 68 Cal.App.3d 201, 215.) 
(86 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. at p. 1 88.) 
Here, Commissioner Painter owns his own consulting finn to assist clients in both the 
political and real estate sectors. He has been retained by Domus in this capacity until December of 
2016. Similar to the situation just described, there is a practical concern that he will be influenced 
by a desire to "maintain ongoing relationships" with Domus, and any other company he represents, 
seeking to contract with any of the relevant government agencies for financing. Indeed, it is difficult 
to imagine how he would ever be able to provide his absolute, undivided allegiance to the 
Commission as a public servant while participating in the contracting process involving his own 
clients. To be sure, a real potential exists for the councilmember to curry favor with his clients 
understanding that it may ultimately affect his ongoing consulting business with them and other 
future business opportunities. This is exactly the type of indirect interest Section 1090 attempts to 
thwart. (See, e.g., 88 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. I 06, I 07 (2005) citing Frazer-Yamor Agency, supra, 68 
Cal.App.3d at pp. 214-215 [California courts strictly enforce Section I 090, and have consistently 
voided contracts where they find the public officer has an indirect interest in the contract; indeed, 
even though an officer may not directly benefit from the tenns of the contract, it is significant that 
the contract will contribute to the financial health of the contracting party with which the officer is 
associated].) 
The conclusion that the councilmember has a financial interest in contracts between the 
relevant government agencies and the clients of his finn is further supported by looking to the 
statutory exceptions for Section I 090. (See 85 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34 (2002) [it is appropriate to 
look to the definitions of the remote and non-interest exceptions contained in sections l 091 and 
l 091.5 for guidance in detennining what falls within the scope of the tenn "financial interest" as 
used in Section I 090].) 
Turning to the statutory exceptions, under Section I 091 (b )(8), an official has a remote 
interest in a contract entered into by the body or board of which they are a member if they are a 
"supplier of goods or services when those goods or services have been supplied to the contracting 
party by the officer for at least five years prior to his or her election or appointment to office." 
Thus, an official would have a financial interest in a contract entered into by a government entity if 
he or she provides services to the party contracting with that government entity, but only a remote 
interest if those services have been provided for at least five years prior to his or her appointment to 
the relevant government entity. 
Here, as mentioned, Commissioner Painter owns a consulting business that provides various 
services to clients in the political and real estate sectors who currently may have or seek to have 
financing contracts with the relevant government agencies. The existence of the instant exception 
further supports the conclusion that he would have a financial interest in all instances where clients 
of his firm seek to enter into contracts with those agencies. We note, however, that because 
Commissioner Painter only began operating his business providing consulting services in August of 
20 I 6, the exception under Section 1091 (b )(8) would not apply. 


File No. A-1 6-104  
Based on the facts provided, Commissioner Painter would have a conflict of interest under 
Section 1 090 involving the recommendation of financing contracts between the relevant 
government agencies and clients of his consulting firm. As mentioned, the remote interest exception 
under Section 1091 (b)(8) does not apply in this situation and we do not find any other exceptions to 
be applicable.3 
Finally, we note that although Commissioner Painter has a conflict of interest in decisions 
involving Domus, and other clients of his firm, the Commission is not prohibited from making 
recommendations on financing so long as Commissioner Painter recuses himself.4 "An advisory 
committee may perform its responsibilities as long as the interested member abstains and does not 
participate in the giving of advice." (82 Ops. Cal. Atty. Gen. 126, 127 ( 1999) citing Cal. Atty. Gen., 
Indexed Letter No. IL 72-143 (Aug. 16, 1 992).)

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Do the conflict of interest provisions under Section 1090 or the Act prohibit a commissioner 
on the Sacramento Housing and Redevelopment Commission ("the Commission") from providing 
recommendations on financing contracts involving clients of his consulting business to specified 
government agencies?

CONCLUSION: Yes. As explained in detail below, Section l 090 prohibits the commissioner from providing 
recommendations on financing contracts involving clients of his consulting business to specified 
government agencies. 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained inFACTS2  
The Sacramento Housing and Redevelopment Agency (the "SHRA") is a joint powers 
authority of the City of Sacramento and the County of Sacramento. The City and County formed the 
SHRA to provide the constituent entities with a common staff and independent agency to oversee 
and implement their housing and redevelopment/community development programs and 
infrastructure. The SHRA owns and manages public housing in Sacramento, administers rental 
assistance vouchers, and uses a number of financing tools to expand housing opportunities. 
The financing tools provided by the SHRA include ''gap financing" which uses federal, 
state, and local funding to provide partial financing to affordable housing and other development 
projects that have already received traditional financing, but require additional funds to complete 
construction. Sources of"gap financing" include HUD HOME funds, HUD CDBG (Community 
Development Block Grant) funds, local housing trust funds from the city and/or county, and/or 
local affordable/mixed income housing funds from both the city and the county. Both the HUD 
Home funds and the HUD CDBG funds are made available to the SHRA annually and must be 
expended within a 24-month period or "funding cycle." If those funds are not expended within the 
funding cycle, they are returned to HUD by the SHRA. 
Funding for affordable housing development is also provided via the housing authorities for 
the City and County of Sacramento, known respectively as the Sacramento City Housing Authority 
and Sacramento County Housing Authority (collectively "housing authority" or "housing 
authorities"). The City Council and Board of Supervisors serve as the respective governing bodies 
for each housing authority. Among other responsibilities, the housing authorities oversee the issuing 
of bonds to provide tax-exempt loans in connection with affordable housing development. While 
both housing authorities exist within the SHRA, they operate independently of the SHRA in regards 
to bond financing. 
The last entity involved in the Sacramento Housing and Redevelopment financing scheme is 
the Sacramento Housing and Redevelopment Commission (the "Commission"). The Commission, 
which consists of eleven appointed members (five are appointed by the City Council and six by the 
Board of Supervisors), serves as the governing body of the SHRA and as an advisory body to the 
housing authorities, the Sacramento City Council, and the Sacramento County Board of 
Supervisors. The Commission has the authority to hire its own personnel, to sue and be sued, to 
receive and expend funds, acquire and dispose of property, and to enter into contracts for staff and 
employee services. 
While the Commission makes recommendations to these various other public agencies, its 
recommendations are not binding. However, the SHRA's staff has reviewed previous past gap 
financing recommendations made by the Commission and subsequent decisions by these other 
public agency boards following the Commission's recommendations. It is SHRA's position that the 
Commission makes substantive recommendations regarding gap financing and that over an 
extended period of time those recommendations have been regularly approved without significant 
amendment or modification by another government agency or agencies. 
1 The following facts are taken largely verbatim from the facts provided in your request and Lance Olson's 
email to the undersigned dated September 7, 2016. 


Approval Process for Bond Financing and "Gap Financing"  
As noted above, both the City and County of Sacramento have various tools for promoting 
affordable housing in the greater Sacramento Region. These tools include the above referenced 
"gap financing" and tax-exempt loans via bond financing. The application and review process for 
"gap financing'' and tax-exempt loans via bond financing are similar, but the processes differ in 
regards to the entities involved. 
For bond financing, the review process begins with the County and City housing authorities. 
Developers seeking tax exempt financing must submit a proposal to the appropriate housing 
authority based on the location of their project. For example, if a developer sought financing for a 
project located in the City of Sacramento, the developer would submit their application to the 
housing authority for the City of Sacramento. 
Once submitted, the relevant housing authority reviews the application and recommends 
whether or not to approve it. If the authority recommends approval, it prepares a financial 
instrument to finance the plan proposed in the application. The financing plan is then referred to the 
Commission for review, and the Commission submits its recommended action to the relevant 
legislative authority - either the City Council or the Board of Supervisors (depending on the 
project's location). 
The relevant legislative authority is not bound by the Commission's recommendation and is 
free to either approve or reject the financing plan. However, the Commission's recommendations 
are given serious consideration by the relevant legislative authorities and have been regularly 
approved. If the plan is approved, the housing authority issues the bond financing and provides 
funding to the applicant for the proposed project. There is no further review or appeal to another 
legislative body if the plan is rejected by the legislative authority. 
For "gap financing," the review process begins with the SHRA. Developers seeking "gap 
financing" must submit an application to the SHRA for review. Once received, an application is 
reviewed by SHRA staff and an initial detennination is made as to whether to recommend 
consideration by the Commission or to reject the application (e.g., if available funds have already 
been committed, then the SHRA staff would reject the application or more likely an application is 
rejected because it is incomplete, or does not comply with the Agency's adopted Multifamily 
Guidelines, or funding requirements). If the application is rejected, it receives no further review. 
Once the Commission reviews the application, its recommended action is provided to the 
relevant legislative authority who is not bound by the Commission's recommendation and is free to 
either approve or reject the financing. However, the Commission's recommendations are given 
serious consideration by the relevant legislative authorities and have been regularly approved by 
them. If financing is approved, the funds are then distributed by the Agency to the applicant. There 
is no further review or appeal to another legislative body if the plan proposal and bond financing are 
rejected by the legislative authority. 


Noah Painter and Domus Development. LLC  
Noah Painter is a member of the Commission. Commissioner Painter also started his own 
consulting finn, as a sole proprietor on August 2, 2016. With his finn, Commissioner Painter has 
the intention of assisting clients in both the political and real estate sectors. Commissioner Painter 
has been retained by Dom us Development, LLC ("Dom us") until December 2016. Dom us is a 
California limited liability company that develops affordable housing and infill mixed-use projects 
throughout California. Commissioner Painter's work with Domus is limited to two projects, one 
located in Sacramento County and the other in Yolo County. The project in Sacramento County will 
not involve financing from the SHRA, but will obtain its financing from state Cap and Trade funds. 
Commissioner Painter has one other client that may, from time to time, have business before the 
SHRA. That client is CFY Development. CFY has retained Commissioner Painter to provide 
advisory services and to assist them in structuring the financing for various projects in their 
portfolio. Some of these projects may come before the SHRA for funding requests. His remaining 
clients involve providing consulting services unrelated to his role on the Commission. 
Before starting his own consulting firm on August 2, 2016, Commissioner Painter was one 
of four Domus employees and served as a project manager for specific affordable housing projects. 
Domus is owned 2/3rds by Newport Partners LLC and I/3rd by Meea Kang (an individual). 
Newport employs approximately 20 people and provides accounting and asset management services 
to Domus. Newport and Ms. Kang also own Domus Management Company, which employs about 
100 people who provide property management services to properties owned by Newport. In effect, 
Newport is the holding company for Domus Development LLC, which acquires and develops 
affordable housing projects for the benefit of Newport. Domus Management Company then 
manages those properties. 
Commissioner Painter has no ownership interest in Domus Development LLC, Newport 
Partners LLC, or Domus Management Company. Domus does not have any funding applications 
currently pending before the Commission. Domus has secured "gap financing" from the SHRA in 
the past for its housing projects. SHRA staff estimate that Domus has sought financing for three 
projects in the past five years and has received HUD Home and HUD CDBG gap financing. 
According to Commissioner Painter, Domus would not likely use or accept in the future gap 
financing from the local housing trust funds or local affordable/mixed income housing funds. In 
addition, Commissioner Painter is not aware of any current plans by Domus to seek gap financing at 
all, but cannot rule out that possibility; thus, SHRA staff assumes it will receive funding 
applications from Domus in the future. Every application is considered on its own merits, and the 
receipt of financing in the past does not guarantee the granting of such funds in the future. 
According to Commissioner Painter, Domus does not intend to utilize bond financing from 
the housing authorities. In the event Domus identifies a project where bond financing makes sense, 
Commissioner Painter believes it will pursue that bond financing from other sources, not the local 
housing authorities.
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 ,J Street• Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 Kara K. Ueda Best Best & Krieger, LLP 500 Capitol Mall, Suite 1700 Sacramento, CA 95814 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-16-104 Dear Ms. Ueda: October 5, 2016 This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Commissioner Noah Painter regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Refonn Act (the "Act") 1 and Section 1090. Please note that we do not advise on any other area of law, including Public Contract Code or common law conflicts of interest. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (111 re Oglesby ( 1975) l FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. In regard to our advice on Section I 090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Sacramento County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section I 097. l(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section l097.l(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section l 090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097. l(c)(S).) QUESTION Do the conflict of interest provisions under Section 1090 or the Act prohibit a commissioner on the Sacramento Housing and Redevelopment Commission ("the Commission") from providing recommendations on financing contracts involving clients of his consulting business to specified government agencies? CONCLUSION Yes. As explained in detail below, Section l 090 prohibits the commissioner from providing recommendations on financing contracts involving clients of his consulting business to specified government agencies. 1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. FACTS2 File No. A-16-104 Page No. 2 The Sacramento Housing and Redevelopment Agency (the "SHRA") is a joint powers authority of the City of Sacramento and the County of Sacramento. The City and County formed the SHRA to provide the constituent entities with a common staff and independent agency to oversee and implement their housing and redevelopment/community development programs and infrastructure. The SHRA owns and manages public housing in Sacramento, administers rental assistance vouchers, and uses a number of financing tools to expand housing opportunities. The financing tools provided by the SHRA include ''gap financing" which uses federal, state, and local funding to provide partial financing to affordable housing and other development projects that have already received traditional financing, but require additional funds to complete construction. Sources of"gap financing" include HUD HOME
